Notes on Switching From Windows 10 to macOS

Recently moved my dev environment to macOS. This post is a few notes on what surprised me in the Windows-to-macOS transition — modifier key remapping, input method switching, full-width vs. half-width characters, trackpad gesture differences, and the reversed scroll direction.

A list of public APIs I've collected for frontend practice projects. Hunting for an API to wire up a small side project takes way longer than it should, so this is a personal cheat sheet — news, audio/video, social, images, lifestyle, and source control. I haven't personally tried all of these; some require auth/OAuth and some have CORS issues, so test before committing.
Turn Off Slack's Auto-Format Messages in One Minute

Recent Slack updates added an auto-formatting feature that converts your Markdown into rendered text in real time as you type. For some people that's nice; for others (myself included) it makes multi-line composition annoying. Two-click fix to turn it off without losing Markdown support entirely.
Markdown Basics: Common Syntax With Examples

A complete walkthrough of common Markdown syntax — from headings, bold, lists to tables and code blocks. Each piece of syntax comes with an example and the corresponding HTML tag, so you can see how Markdown maps to semantic HTML. Useful both for writing better content and for SEO awareness.
The Art of Text Communication Every Software Engineer Should Learn

A few words don't sound like much, but the way you write them changes how they land — and for software engineers, who do most of their communication through text (Slack, Discord, email), this matters a lot. This post covers what trips people up most in text conversations: over-punctuation, private abbreviations, modal particles, and emojis. Examples include a real boss-misread case from a friend, and the small adjustments that shift tone without rewriting your sentence.
Customize Windows Terminal with oh-my-posh (PowerShell Prompt + Windows Terminal Setup)

Staring at the same plain PowerShell prompt all day gets old. This post walks through giving Windows PowerShell a git-style prompt with oh-my-posh, plus installing and customizing Windows Terminal (the newer Microsoft terminal) — themes, fonts, background image. Heads up — this article was written in 2019; oh-my-posh has since moved to v3+ which uses different config commands.

A walkthrough of Slack basics — how to join a Slack workspace, download the desktop client, navigate the UI (channels, direct messages, threads), and the everyday features you'll actually use (editing/deleting messages, threading, tagging, emoji reactions, code blocks, Markdown). For anyone joining a team that uses Slack for the first time.
User Stories from a Frontend Dev's Perspective

A follow-up after running through User Stories on a real project — the mindset really is opposite to traditional backend planning. Backend devs typically start with "what features and database columns do we need?" but User Stories start with "what does the user need?". This post is my notes from the first time I lived through that shift on a job-board example.

An interviewer asked me about User Stories and I realized I wasn't super clear on it — so I dug in and wrote a short note. A User Story captures a feature from the user's perspective ("As a [user], I want [something] so that [reason]") rather than from a database or implementation perspective. This post is the basics — the formula, a few real examples, and how it shifts how you think about building features.
